lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying expanded or extruded polystyrene foam insulation, protective packaging, cold-chain containers, food-service items where lawful, and other molded or cut foam products to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive foam-molding units, distributors and fabricators without primary foam manufacturing, non-polystyrene foam makers, resin producers, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales under purchase orders, project quotes, distributor arrangements, and supply agreements, priced by unit, board, volume, weight, or run; the mix spans commodity foodware and insulation through custom protective packaging and molded components.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages for all NAICS 326100 plastics-product manufacturing, not 326140 or task exposure. I map portions of quoting, design, order entry, planning, quality inspection, molding and extrusion monitoring, maintenance, packing, material movement, and administration to AI capability and weight toward the production payroll. Foam converting and molding may use more manual packing and handling than some continuous plastics processes, but much of that work requires robotics rather than AI alone; counting only AI-addressable fractions avoids treating whole occupations as substitutable.
a.rationale: The BLS plastics-product mix is dominated by production, machine, assembly, packaging, and material-moving roles, with much smaller management and business shares. AI can improve visual inspection, tool and package design, scheduling, demand forecasting, maintenance, documentation, and customer service. Blocking, molding, cutting, tool changes, packing, warehousing, and maintenance remain physical, and some line control is already automated.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study in EPS and XPS plants covering molding and extrusion, cutting, packaging, warehousing, tooling, quality, maintenance, planning, design, sales, and administration, explicitly measuring the existing automation baseline.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than 326140.
Task shares are inferred rather than observed.
Insulation board, custom protective packaging, and foodware plants have materially different occupation mixes.

rho.rationale: Computer vision, predictive maintenance, nesting and design assistance, production planning, order/document automation, and demand forecasting can be implemented within five years. Conversion is limited by legacy equipment, weak data capture, changing product geometries, small-firm integration capacity, the need for physical automation around handling, safety and fire-performance requirements, and human quality release.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level deployment studies reporting sensor and MES coverage, AI pilot conversion, labor hours avoided, scrap and downtime changes, image-inspection accuracy, robotics dependencies, integration cost, and customer-approval requirements.
caveats: No five-year adoption measure specific to polystyrene foam manufacturing was located.
Many apparent AI opportunities require conveyors, robots, or tooling changes, not software alone.

e.rationale: Independent foam manufacturers usually supply repeat external customers and qualify. Exclusions include captive packaging operations, pure converters or distributors, firms dominated by prohibited or rapidly shrinking food-service lines without transferable diversification, distressed plants, and records whose true primary process is non-polystyrene foam.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the 113 frozen candidates verifying resin and process, independent ownership, external revenue, repeat-order share, product/end-market mix, regulatory exposure, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and transferable facilities and permits.
caveats: Eligibility is estimated and the frozen firm count is margin-bridged.
Public NAICS records often do not reveal the split between insulation, protective packaging, cold chain, foodware, and converting.

s5.rationale: A low-twenties five-year probability reflects normal private-company succession and consolidation, reduced for family/internal transfers, indefinite holds, minority recapitalizations, closures, and failed processes. Regulatory pressure may prompt some owners to sell but may instead cause product-line closure or capex avoidance, which is not a qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, and packaging-industry broker records of U.S. EPS/XPS control transactions from 2016-2025, matched to eligible firms, plus owner-age, succession, and regulatory-capex interviews.
caveats: No six-digit transfer-rate or owner-age data was found.
Public transaction coverage is biased toward larger strategic acquisitions.

q.rationale: Custom protective packaging, tooling, geographic freight economics, cold-chain qualification, and service reliability provide switching friction and allow some savings retention. Commodity insulation boards and food-service products are price-transparent, while resin and freight pass-through, distributor power, project bidding, and customer cost-down demands return gains to buyers.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bid histories, and invoices showing resin/freight escalators, fixed-price duration, tooling ownership, annual cost-downs, distributor rebates, rebid frequency, customer concentration, and margin retention after process changes.
caveats: Retention varies sharply between commodity foodware or board and engineered protective packaging or qualified cold-chain products.

d5.bridge: BLS projects all plastics-product real output to grow 1.1% annually from 2024 to 2034, while DOE notes insulation as part of building-envelope energy performance and BLS projects construction employment growth. These are positive proxies, not 326140 volume forecasts. In the opposite direction, Washington bans EPS food-service products, coolers, and loose-fill packaging and Oregon bans foam food containers and packing peanuts; California imposes escalating recycling thresholds. Because the code mixes insulated construction and protective packaging with legally pressured single-use products, I set the center below the broad-plastics five-year implication and use a wide range. State restrictions may overstate national decline but can diffuse to other jurisdictions.
d5.rationale: Insulation, protective packaging, appliances, cold chain, and construction support durable demand, and broad plastics output is expected to grow modestly. Food-service and loose-fill uses face explicit bans and recycling requirements, while fiber, molded pulp, alternative foams, reusable packaging, and other insulation materials compete. The net five-year quantity outlook is therefore near flat with material downside and upside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. shipment forecasts and producer volume histories for EPS/XPS split among insulation, protective packaging, cold chain, appliances, food service, and other uses, overlaid with enacted state rules and customer substitution plans.
caveats: State bans cover selected products and do not prohibit all polystyrene foam.
The industry product mix is unknown, so regulatory effects cannot be precisely weighted.

o.rationale: Remaining demand for the current foam basket requires a physical manufacturer to expand or extrude resin, mold or cut products, test performance, and deliver bulky goods. Software cannot supply it. A residual share can disappear through customer redesign, reusable systems, molded fiber, alternative insulation or foams, and in-house protective-packaging fabrication; those substitution risks are partly reflected in d5 but also affect who supplies year-five quantity.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with packaging engineers, food-service distributors, insulation contractors, cold-chain users, and OEMs about redesign, reusable systems, insourcing, material substitution, and approved-supplier requirements.
caveats: Care is needed not to double-count regulatory volume loss already included in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Polystyrene foam manufacturing has repeat external demand and useful AI opportunities around quality, maintenance, design, planning, and administration, but the industry combines relatively durable insulation and protective packaging with single-use products under active regulatory pressure. Product mix is therefore decisive.
narrative.ai_impact: DOE identifies automated visual quality control, characterization, operations optimization, and predictive maintenance as feasible manufacturing uses. Foam producers can also use AI for package design, nesting, quoting, forecasting, scheduling, and documentation. Savings are constrained where work is physical or requires robotics, and by legacy controls, limited plant data, geometry changes, fire and thermal standards, and human quality accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Custom tooling, freight radius, protective-performance design, and cold-chain qualification can create switching costs. Commodity boards, coolers, cups, trays, and standard packaging are more price-sensitive, and resin or freight escalators reveal cost changes. Retention is therefore moderate and highly dependent on product differentiation.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independent manufacturers with repeat external customers fit the lens, but candidates need product-level diligence. A business concentrated in prohibited foodware may be economically or operationally nontransferable even if its NAICS code fits. Succession and consolidation create sale opportunities, while closures and product-line exits do not count as transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Broad plastics output and construction-linked insulation provide support, but Washington and Oregon already prohibit specified EPS food-service and loose-fill items, and California requires escalating recycling performance for EPS foodware. These measures do not ban insulation or most protective packaging, yet they demonstrate real substitution and regulatory risk. Stable overall quantity is plausible only if durable segments offset single-use erosion.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Six-digit occupation and end-market data are missing. The same code covers businesses with very different regulatory exposure, pricing power, automation, freight economics, and capex. Material substitution, fire and building codes, resin volatility, recycling mandates, customer concentration, energy cost, and bulky-product logistics can overwhelm AI savings. A foodware-heavy plant could be a poor transfer candidate despite good operating improvement potential.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable insulation and engineered protective-packaging demand supports AI-enabled quality, uptime, and planning improvements.
narrative.principal_weakness: Single-use EPS bans and substitution make product mix a first-order risk, while much plant labor remains physical.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 326100 Plastics Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Broad plastics-product occupational and wage mix, including total employment and production, management, business, extrusion, molding, assembly, packing, maintenance, and material-moving roles.

sources.title: AI for Energy: Opportunities for a Modern Grid and Clean Energy Economy
sources.supports: DOE describes AI manufacturing uses including automated visual quality control, advanced characterization, operations optimization, predictive maintenance with IoT sensors, and material sorting.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects NAICS 326100 real output from $190.7 billion in 2024 to $212.4 billion in 2034 at 1.1% annually; construction employment grows 4.4%, building construction 4.6%, and insulation-contractor employment 2.5%.

sources.title: Expanded Polystyrene Ban - Washington State Department of Ecology
sources.supports: Washington bans EPS packing peanuts from June 2023 and portable coolers and food-service products including containers, plates, bowls, clamshells, trays, and cups from June 2024, while listing exemptions for certain protective and raw-food packaging.

sources.title: Foodware Containers and Polystyrene Foam - Oregon Department of Environmental Quality
sources.supports: Effective January 1, 2025, Oregon prohibits food vendors from using polystyrene foam containers for prepared food and prohibits sales of foam containers and packing peanuts, while exempting most protective shipping material.

sources.title: California SB 54: Solid Waste, Packaging, and Plastic Food Service Ware
sources.supports: California law requires demonstrated EPS food-service recycling rates of at least 25% from 2025, 30% from 2028, 50% from 2030, and 65% from 2032 for continued sale or distribution.
